refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency compatible with safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR is already focused on a single obligation: ensuring teleoperation latency is compatible with safe remote operation. It does not combine multiple requirements or specify implementation details, thus it is atomic and solution-neutral.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure the end-to-end communication latency during teleoperation under agreed network conditions and verify that the latency meets the safety compatibility criteria, such as p95 latency ≤ 150 ms.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency compatible with safe remote operation.

candidate_dps: Implement a dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation
Design a real-time data compression and prioritization mechanism to minimize transmission delays
Establish a distributed control architecture with local autonomy to reduce dependency on end-to-end latency
Incorporate predictive control algorithms to compensate for communication delays
Use a modular communication interface that can adapt to varying network conditions
selected_dp.selected_dp: Implement a dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation

potential_sources: Tight integration between communication protocol and control system
Dependency on network conditions affecting latency guarantees
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP focuses on the communication protocol layer, which logically isolates latency management from control algorithms and hardware, supporting independence and modularity.

assumptions_risks: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters to meet latency targets
Protocol optimization alone can achieve p95 ≤ 150 ms latency without excessive complexity
Operational policies do not impose additional latency constraints beyond network conditions
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Consider explicitly including adaptive mechanisms in the DP to handle variable network conditions and ensure latency targets are consistently met under real-world scenarios.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Module responsible for enabling remote control and communication with the vehicle, including teleoperation-specific communication protocols.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bi-directional flow of telemetry data between vehicle and teleoperation module.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands and control instructions sent from teleoperation to vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical and logical network interface used for communication between teleoperation and vehicle.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is a broad system-level module; containing the DP here would cause significant diffusion across unrelated subsystems.
Vehicle module includes hardware and embedded systems; implementing the protocol stack here risks diffusion into hardware-specific components and reduces modularity.
Teleoperation is the most specific module focused on remote control and communication, making it highly cohesive for the DP with minimal diffusion.
Safety Services focus on safety alerts and monitoring, not communication protocol implementation, leading to poor containment and high diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most appropriate boundary to contain the dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack because it directly manages remote control communication and can encapsulate the protocol logic with minimal coupling to other modules.
containment_risks: Potential overlap with Vehicle module if low-level network interfaces are tightly coupled.
Risk of protocol changes impacting Tasking and Telemetry interfaces requiring coordination.
If Safety Services require real-time alerts over the same protocol, cross-module dependencies may increase.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: If containment risks increase, consider refining the DP to separate protocol stack implementation from physical network interface handling, assigning the latter to Vehicle and the protocol logic to Teleoperation for clearer boundaries.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly addresses the FR by specifying a dedicated low-latency communication protocol stack optimized for teleoperation, which is necessary to achieve the required end-to-end latency (p95 ≤ 150 ms) for safe remote operation.

diffusion_risks: Potential overlap with Vehicle module if low-level network interfaces are tightly coupled.
Risk of protocol changes impacting Tasking and Telemetry interfaces requiring coordination.
If Safety Services require real-time alerts over the same protocol, cross-module dependencies may increase.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The Teleoperation module has the highest containment score (9) and lowest diffusion score (2), indicating it is the most cohesive and appropriate boundary for implementing the DP. This minimizes diffusion risk and supports modularity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of the FR through the IM is feasible by measuring the end-to-end communication latency within the Teleoperation module under agreed network conditions to ensure p95 ≤ 150 ms.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of the DP to the Teleoperation IM is clear and well-justified, with explicit interfaces defined for Telemetry, Tasking, and Network Link that support the required functionality.

recommended_revisions: Monitor and manage the potential overlap with the Vehicle module by clearly separating physical network interface handling (assigned to Vehicle) from protocol stack implementation (assigned to Teleoperation).
Coordinate changes impacting Tasking and Telemetry interfaces to minimize cross-module dependencies.
If Safety Services require real-time alerts over the same protocol, consider defining clear interface contracts or separate protocol handling to reduce coupling.
Refine the DP if necessary to separate protocol logic from physical network interface handling to maintain clear module boundaries and reduce diffusion risk.

decision.justification: The current DP and IM adequately address the FR with clear allocation, feasible verification, and manageable diffusion risks. The gates confirm that the DP directly satisfies the FR, the IM is an appropriate module boundary with high containment and low diffusion, and verification is feasible. Recommended revisions focus on managing interface overlaps and coordination rather than requiring further decomposition. Given the high safety criticality, maintaining clear module boundaries and monitoring risks is sufficient without further sub-FR derivation at this stage.

revise.why: To refine the DP by separating protocol logic from physical network interface handling and to manage diffusion risks by clarifying module boundaries and interface contracts.

assumptions: The Teleoperation module can maintain clear separation from the Vehicle module regarding network interfaces.
Coordination among Tasking, Telemetry, and Safety Services teams is feasible to manage interface dependencies.
open_risks: Potential overlap with Vehicle module in physical network interface handling.
Cross-module dependencies due to protocol changes impacting Tasking and Telemetry interfaces.
Increased coupling if Safety Services require real-time alerts over the same protocol.
evidence_needed: Verification results of end-to-end communication latency within the Teleoperation module under agreed network conditions.
Monitoring reports on interface separation and diffusion risk management during implementation.
